An Intelligent System for Academic Manuscript
Formatting and Compliance
A project report submitted in partial fulfillment of the requirements
for the award of the degree of
B. Tech. in Computer Science and Engineering
By
Aarushi Jain (2022UCS1591)

DELHI-110078
MARCH 2026

CERTIFICATE
This is to certify that the project titled An Intelligent System for Academic Manuscript
Formatting and Compliance is a bonafide record of the work done by
Aarushi Jain (2022UCS1591)
Aayushi Jain (2022UCS1606)
Aarushi Kushwaha (2022UCS1615)
under my supervision and guidance in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the award of
the degree of Bachelor of Technology in Computer Science and Engineering of the Netaji
Subhas University of Technology, DELHI-110078, during the year 2025 - 2026.
Their work is genuine and has not been submitted for the award of any other degree to the
best of my knowledge.
DATE:
Dr. Vijay Kumar Bohat
Assistant Professor
Division of Computer Science
Netaji Subhas University of Technology
1

DECLARATION
This is to certify that the work which is being hereby presented by us in this project titled
“An Intelligent System for Academic Manuscript Formatting and Compliance” in partial
fulfilment of the award of the Bachelor of Technology submitted at the Department of Computer
Science and Engineering, Netaji Subhas University of Technology, New Delhi, is a genuine
account of our work carried out during the period from December 2025 to February 2026
under the guidance of Dr. Vijay Kumar Bohat, Department of Computer Science, Netaji
Subhas University of Technology, New Delhi.
The matter embodied in the project report to the best of our knowledge has not been sub-
mitted for the award of any other degree elsewhere.
DATE:
Aarushi Jain
Aayushi Jain
Aarushi Kushwaha
(2022UCS1591)
(2022UCS1606)
(2022UCS1615)
2

ACKNOWLEDGEMENT
We would like to express our sincere gratitude to Dr. Vijay Kumar Bohat, Department of
Computer Science and Engineering, Netaji Subhas University of Technology, Delhi, for his in-
valuable guidance and support throughout the brainstorming, theoretical framework, and partial
implementation of this project. His insights, encouragement, and constructive feedback greatly
enhanced our understanding and helped shape the direction of this work.
We are also thankful for the environment of learning and collaboration provided by the de-
partment, which allowed us to explore ideas freely and translate them into practical outcomes.
3

5

Introduction
Fix my Paper is a web-based intelligent academic formatting verification platform that is de-
signed to automatically analyze research papers and detect structural, syntactic, citation, and
formatting-related errors. Academic writing in technical domains follows strict formatting stan-
dards prescribed by institutions and journals like IEEE, APA etc. These standards ensure clar-
ity, reproducibility, and professional presentation of research work.
However, undergraduate students often lack formal training in academic writing conven-
tions. While they may possess strong technical knowledge, they frequently make formatting
mistakes such as:
• Improper ordering of figures and tables
• Missing figure/table captions
• Equations without numbering or explanation
• Incorrect citation styles
• Mismatched in-text citations and reference lists
• Inconsistent typography and notation
Most of the writing tools that currently exist primarily focus on grammar and language
improvement, offering little support for technical formatting compliance. Fix my Paper helps
bridge this gap by integrating rule-based validation and machine learning techniques to generate
structured, actionable improvement reports within seconds.
6

Motivation
The motivation for developing our project arises from the growing importance of academic
writing quality in technical education. Students with technical backgrounds are increasingly
required to produce research papers, project reports, and theses that adhere to strict formatting
standards defined by academic institutions and journals. While students often possess strong
technical knowledge, they frequently lack adequate training in academic writing conventions,
resulting in structural and formatting inconsistencies that negatively affect the clarity and pro-
fessionalism of their work.
A key motivating factor is the limited time available for faculty members to provide detailed
formatting feedback. Professors must manage multiple responsibilities, including teaching, re-
search, and administrative duties, which restricts their ability to thoroughly review formatting
aspects of every student submission. As a result, many formatting errors persist across sub-
missions, slowing students’ development of academic writing skills and increasing the overall
review burden.
Additionally, existing writing support tools primarily focus on grammar correction and lan-
guage enhancement, offering minimal assistance for technical formatting issues . Tools that
do address citation management often operate independently and do not verify contextual cor-
rectness within the document. This fragmented support creates a gap in automated academic
quality assurance for technical papers.
The need for an affordable and student-friendly automated solution further strengthens the
motivation behind this project. Many available platforms are subscription-based and may not
be accessible to all students. Therefore, a lightweight and specialized platform capable of
delivering fast, actionable feedback on technical formatting can significantly improve student
productivity, reduce faculty workload, and enhance overall research paper quality.
7

Literature Review
0.1
Literature Review
The domain of academic writing assistance has evolved significantly with the advancement
of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and machine learning techniques. Existing solutions
in this area can broadly be categorized into three groups: grammar and language correction
tools, plagiarism detection systems, and citation/reference management software. While these
systems contribute to improving academic writing quality, they operate in isolation and do not
provide comprehensive structural and formatting compliance verification tailored for technical
research papers.
0.1.1
Grammar and Language Correction Tools
Grammar correction platforms such as Grammarly, LanguageTool, and Writefull utilize NLP-
based models trained on large textual corpora to detect grammatical, lexical, and stylistic er-
rors. Recent research in Grammatical Error Correction (GEC) demonstrates the effectiveness
of transformer-based models in improving sentence fluency and readability.
However, these tools primarily treat documents as unstructured text and focus on linguistic
refinement rather than academic formatting compliance. They do not verify figure and table
sequencing, caption placement, equation numbering consistency, citation-reference mapping,
DOI validation, or journal-specific structural guidelines. Consequently, while they improve lan-
guage quality, they fail to address technical formatting requirements essential for engineering
research documents.
8

0.1.2
Plagiarism Detection Systems
Plagiarism detection systems compare textual similarity against large academic databases to
ensure originality. These platforms play a critical role in maintaining academic integrity. How-
ever, their objective is limited to similarity detection and originality verification.
They do not analyze structural correctness, formatting consistency, citation integrity, equa-
tion referencing, or document layout compliance. Therefore, plagiarism tools cannot serve as
comprehensive academic formatting verification systems.
0.1.3
Citation and Reference Management Tools
Reference management tools such as Zotero and Mendeley assist researchers in generating
citations in various formats, including IEEE and APA styles. These tools simplify bibliography
creation and reference organization.
Despite their usefulness, they operate as standalone utilities and do not verify contextual
correctness within the document. They do not ensure one-to-one mapping between in-text cita-
tions and reference list entries, detect missing references, validate DOI availability, check order-
ing consistency, or enforce journal-specific formatting constraints across the entire manuscript.
0.1.4
Research Gap Identification
A comprehensive review of existing literature and tools reveals a critical gap: there is no
lightweight, integrated system specifically designed to perform structural validation, citation
verification, equation compliance checks, figure-table consistency validation, and typography
analysis within a single unified platform.
Most available systems focus on isolated aspects of writing improvement rather than holistic
academic compliance verification. This limitation creates the need for an intelligent, automated
solution capable of analyzing technical manuscripts as structured academic artifacts rather than
plain text documents.
0.1.5
Comparative Analysis with Existing Solutions
Table 1 presents a structured comparison between existing tools and the proposed system.

0.1.6
Discussion
The comparative evaluation clearly demonstrates that existing tools operate in silos, address-
ing either grammar correction, citation generation, or originality verification independently.
None provide an integrated compliance framework capable of analyzing structural formatting,
citation integrity, equation consistency, and contextual integration simultaneously.
The proposed system differentiates itself by treating academic manuscripts as structured
technical documents. By combining rule-based validation mechanisms with machine learning-
based contextual verification, the system delivers comprehensive, fast, and actionable compli-
ance feedback tailored specifically for engineering and scientific research papers.
10

Problem Statement
Undergraduate and postgraduate students in technical disciplines are required to prepare re-
search papers and project reports that follow strict academic formatting standards prescribed
by universities, conferences, and journals. However, many students lack formal training in
technical writing and formatting conventions, which results in frequent structural and stylistic
errors in their submissions. These errors include improper figure and table referencing, miss-
ing or incorrectly placed captions, unnumbered or unexplained equations, inconsistent citation
styles, and mismatches between in-text citations and the reference list.
The absence of timely and detailed feedback further aggravates the problem. Faculty mem-
bers often face a heavy workload and are unable to provide comprehensive formatting correc-
tions for every submission. As a result, students repeatedly make similar mistakes, leading to
poor document quality, increased review cycles, and delays in evaluation or publication.
Existing writing assistance tools primarily focus on grammar and language improvement
and offer limited support for technical formatting verification. Tools that handle citations or
references work in isolation and do not validate the overall structural consistency of the doc-
ument. Consequently, there is a lack of an integrated, automated solution that can analyze
multiple components of a technical paper, including text structure, figures, tables, equations,
and references.
Therefore, there is a need for a comprehensive and automated system that can accurately
detect formatting inconsistencies, structural errors, and reference-related issues in technical
academic documents and provide actionable feedback to help students improve the quality and
compliance of their research papers.
11

Objectives
The primary objective of our project is to develop an intelligent, automated system capable of
verifying the structural and formatting correctness of technical research papers. The platform is
designed to go beyond traditional grammar-checking tools by focusing specifically on academic
formatting standards required in engineering and scientific writing. The purpose of this project
is to ensure that research documents comply with institutional and journal-specific guidelines
prior to submission.
Figure 1: System Architecture of Fix my Paper
One of the key objectives is to automatically detect formatting inconsistencies related to
figures, tables, and equations. This includes verifying whether figures and tables are properly
numbered, correctly referenced in the text, placed after their first mention, and accompanied
by appropriate captions. For mathematical expressions, the system aims to check sequential
equation numbering, proper formatting of variables and units, and whether all symbols are
defined clearly within the text.
12

Another important objective is to validate citation and reference integrity. The system seeks
to ensure that in-text citations correctly correspond to entries in the reference list, that refer-
ences are complete and properly ordered, and that citation styles such as IEEE or APA are
consistently applied. By automating these checks, the platform reduces the likelihood of sub-
mission rejections due to referencing errors.
The project also aims to reduce manual correction effort for both students and faculty mem-
bers. By generating structured and actionable feedback reports within a short processing time,
the system encourages students to independently refine their work before submission. This
objective directly contributes to improving overall academic writing quality and promoting
self-learning.
From a methodological perspective, the system follows a structured processing pipeline.
The process begins when a user uploads a research paper in PDF or DOC format. The backend
then extracts textual content, figures, tables, equations, and references using document parsing
utilities.
Following extraction, a rule-based engine employing regular expressions and pattern-matching
techniques analyzes structural formatting aspects. These rules are designed to detect errors such
as skipped numbering sequences, incorrect caption placement, improper punctuation, inconsis-
tent typography, and citation bracket formatting issues.
In parallel, machine learning models are applied to analyze the references section more
intelligently. These models assist in identifying incomplete references, mismatches between
in-text citations and bibliography entries, DOI validation issues, and inconsistencies in citation
style formatting. The combination of rule-based logic and machine learning enhances both
accuracy and contextual understanding.
Finally, the detected issues are aggregated into a structured analysis report. The report cat-
egorizes errors by severity and type, provides specific suggestions for correction, and may in-
clude an overall formatting compliance score. This report is then displayed through an intuitive
web interface, allowing users to easily review and implement the suggested improvements.
Through this systematic objective-driven methodology, our project aims to deliver a com-
prehensive, efficient, and user-friendly solution for technical academic formatting verification.
13

Simulation Platform and Requirements
The simulation platform for the project is designed to replicate a real-world academic docu-
ment processing environment where research papers are uploaded, analyzed, and evaluated for
formatting compliance.
The validation checks implemented in the simulation environment are listed in Table 2.
Table 2: Stage-1 Validation Checks Used in Simulation
#

2

The frontend of the platform is built using modern web technologies such as React.js or
Next.js, combined with CSS for responsive and clean user interface design. The purpose of the
frontend is to provide a smooth document upload experience, display structured error reports,
and ensure intuitive navigation. The interface is designed to clearly categorize detected issues
under sections such as figures, equations, citations, references, and typography, making it easy
for users to understand and correct errors.
15

The backend of the system is implemented using Node.js or Python with FastAPI. The
backend handles core processing tasks, including document parsing, rule-based validation, ma-
chine learning inference, and report generation. FastAPI is particularly suitable due to its high
performance, asynchronous capabilities, and compatibility with machine learning workflows.
The backend acts as the central processing unit of the platform, coordinating data extraction,
analysis, and structured output generation.
For document analysis and machine learning operations, we have used Python as the pri-
mary programming language due to its rich ecosystem of libraries. Regular expressions and
rule-based engines are employed to detect formatting patterns such as figure numbering, cita-
tion brackets, equation formatting, and punctuation consistency. In addition, Natural Language
Processing techniques and Scikit-learn models assist in more complex tasks such as citation-
reference matching, reference completeness verification, and DOI validation. This hybrid ap-
proach ensures both speed and contextual intelligence in error detection.
The system requires a reliable database layer, such as PostgreSQL or MongoDB, to manage
user session data, analysis results, and system logs temporarily. While uploaded documents are
not stored permanently in order to maintain privacy, temporary storage ensures smooth report
generation and retrieval. Data deletion policies are implemented to remove documents after
processing, ensuring compliance with privacy and academic integrity standards.
From an infrastructure perspective, the platform is deployed on a cloud-based virtual ma-
chine capable of handling document uploads and concurrent processing requests. Containeriza-
tion using Docker ensures portability and consistent deployment across environments. Object
storage is utilized for temporary file management, while monitoring and logging services main-
tain system reliability, uptime tracking, and performance diagnostics.
For machine learning model training and experimentation, a short-term GPU-enabled com-
pute instance may be required. This is particularly useful during development and optimization
phases to improve model accuracy in reference classification and pattern recognition. Once
trained, inference can be performed efficiently on standard CPU-based servers to reduce oper-
ational costs.
Security and accessibility are also essential requirements of the platform. A registered do-
16

main name and SSL certificate are necessary to ensure secure HTTPS communication between
users and the server. Authentication mechanisms can be integrated in later stages to support
institutional adoption and personalized report tracking.
Overall, the simulation platform integrates modern web technologies, cloud infrastructure,
and machine learning frameworks to create a scalable and efficient academic formatting veri-
fication system. The combination of frontend usability, backend computational efficiency, and
secure deployment ensures that the system can operate reliably under real-world academic us-
age conditions.
17

Proposed Method
The proposed system is designed to automatically analyze technical research documents and
identify structural, formatting, grammatical, and reference-related issues using a hybrid archi-
tecture that combines rule-based validation, regular expressions, Python-based logical checks,
Optical Character Recognition (OCR), and machine learning models. The complete methodol-
ogy is described below.
0.1.7
Document Upload and Input Handling
The process begins with the user uploading a research paper through a web-based interface.
The system accepts documents in PDF or DOC format. Once uploaded, the file is securely
stored and passed to the backend processing pipeline. The interface is designed to be intuitive
and efficient, enabling rapid submission and automated evaluation.
0.1.8
OCR-Based Text Extraction and JSON Structuring
To ensure accurate structural analysis, the system implements an OCR-based processing pipeline
for PDF documents.
• The uploaded PDF is first processed using OCR to extract textual and layout information.
• The extracted content is then converted into a structured JSON representation containing
hierarchical components such as headings, paragraphs, figures, tables, equations, cap-
tions, citations, and references.
• This JSON structure preserves positional metadata (page number, bounding boxes, or-
dering), enabling precise structural validation.
This intermediate JSON representation serves as the core data structure for downstream
validation modules.
18

0.1.9
Structural and Formatting Validation Using Regex and Python Checks
The system applies a rule-based validation engine primarily implemented using:
• Regular Expressions (Regex)
• Python-based deterministic logical checks
Regex-based validation is used for detecting:
• Figure and table numbering patterns
• Caption placement (above/below validation)
• Equation numbering formats
• Citation bracket structure (e.g., [1], (1))
• Typography consistency patterns
Python logical checks ensure:
• Sequential numbering without skipping
• One-to-one mapping between mentions and objects
• Correct ordering of figures and tables after first reference
• Presence of defined symbols and units
This rule-driven approach ensures strict compliance with deterministic academic formatting
standards.
0.1.10
Machine Learning-Based Citation and Reference Analysis
While structural components rely primarily on regex and rule-based checks, reference valida-
tion is handled using a machine learning model.
The ML model performs:
• Reference completeness verification (authors, title, venue, year, pages)
19

• DOI detection and validation pattern recognition
• Citation style classification (IEEE, APA, etc.)
• Detection of mismatches between in-text citations and bibliography entries
• Identification of incomplete or inconsistent references
The model is trained to recognize structured reference patterns and contextual inconsisten-
cies that cannot be reliably captured using rule-based methods alone. This makes the reference
validation module the most ML-intensive component of the system.
0.1.11
Grammar and Syntax Correction Module
In addition to formatting compliance, the system incorporates grammar and syntax correction.
This module applies NLP-based correction techniques to detect:
• Grammatical inconsistencies
• Sentence-level syntax errors
• Punctuation errors
• Improper academic tone structures
The integration of grammar validation ensures that the final manuscript satisfies both struc-
tural compliance and professional language standards.
0.1.12
Contextual and Multi-Modal Validation
The system performs contextual validation to ensure that technical elements are properly inte-
grated within the manuscript. It verifies:
• Whether every equation is referenced in surrounding text
• Whether figures and tables are explained contextually
• Whether acronyms are defined at first occurrence
This stage moves beyond pattern matching and evaluates semantic integration within the
document structure.
20

0.1.13
Annotated PDF Reconstruction
After all validations are completed:
• The structured JSON representation is enriched with detected error metadata.
• The system reprocesses the document using OCR-based positional mapping.
• An annotated PDF is generated by overlaying highlights and comments at precise coor-
dinates corresponding to detected violations.
This PDF →JSON →Annotated PDF pipeline ensures traceability between detected issues
and their exact visual location in the manuscript.
0.1.14
Report Generation and Feedback
The system generates a structured compliance report categorizing errors by:
• Category (Figures, Tables, Equations, Citations, References, Typography, Grammar)
• Severity level
• Corrective recommendation
The analysis process is optimized for performance and produces results within approxi-
mately 40 seconds per document under standard computational settings.
0.1.15
System Benefits
The hybrid architecture combining:
• Regex-based rule validation
• Python logical checks
• Machine learning-based reference intelligence
• OCR-based structured reconstruction
21

ensures high precision, contextual reliability, and scalability. The system is lightweight,
cloud-deployable, and cost-effective for students. It significantly reduces manual formatting
effort while minimizing repetitive faculty workload by automating non-conceptual compliance
verification tasks.
22

Conclusion
The project “An Intelligent System for Academic Manuscript Formatting and Compliance” has
successfully progressed from conceptual design to a fully functional and deployable prototype.
The system is now operational in a real-world environment, with the frontend successfully
deployed on Vercel and the backend deployed on Render, ensuring scalability, reliability, and
secure cloud-based access.
To validate real-world applicability, the system was rigorously tested on 20 research pa-
pers sourced from leading Indian Institutes of Technology (IITs). The testing phase confirmed
that the platform effectively detects structural inconsistencies, citation mismatches, equation
formatting issues, figure-table misplacement, and typography errors in technical manuscripts.
The evaluation demonstrated that the system is capable of automatically identifying and report-
ing formatting violations across all 30 validation checks listed in the simulation framework.
A major milestone achieved during this phase is that the system currently eliminates all
grammar and syntax errors identified in the validation table, thereby ensuring that submitted
manuscripts meet professional writing standards in addition to structural compliance. This
significantly strengthens the practical reliability of the platform.
Furthermore, the project has received 25,000 rupees in research funding from Netaji Subhas
University of Technology (NSUT) to support advanced prototype development, model refine-
ment, and scalability enhancements. This institutional backing validates both the technical
feasibility and academic significance of the system.
The hybrid architecture combining rule-based validation with machine learning models has
proven to be highly effective. Rule-based logic ensures strict adherence to deterministic aca-
demic standards (numbering sequences, caption positioning, citation brackets), while machine
learning models intelligently validate contextual relationships such as citation-reference map-
23

ping, DOI verification, and reference completeness.
From an infrastructure perspective, successful cloud deployment confirms that the system is
production-ready and capable of handling real-time document analysis requests. The process-
ing time remains optimized at approximately 40 seconds per document, demonstrating both
efficiency and scalability.
Key Outcomes Achieved
• Technical feasibility through successful cloud deployment
• Functional reliability through IIT paper testing
• Writing accuracy through grammar and syntax elimination
• Institutional validation through research funding support
• Scalability ensured through production-ready infrastructure
With continued refinement and expanded dataset validation, the system has strong poten-
tial for institutional integration within universities as a mandatory pre-submission compliance
tool. In the long term, the platform can evolve into a standardized academic quality assurance
framework capable of improving research submission standards at scale.
Prototype Demonstration
Figure 2, Figure 3 and Figure 4 illustrate the deployed prototype interface and the structured
compliance report generated by the system.
Prototype Demonstration and System Output
To validate the practical implementation of the proposed framework, the deployed prototype
was evaluated through real-time document uploads and automated compliance analysis. The
system provides a structured multi-stage output consisting of document-level validation, de-
tailed categorized error explanations, and an annotated PDF highlighting detected violations.
24

Figure 2 illustrates the initial system response upon document upload. The interface presents
a high-level compliance summary, categorizes detected issues (figures, equations, citations, ref-
erences, typography), and generates an overall formatting assessment score. This stage con-
firms successful parsing, structural extraction, and rule-engine execution.
Figure 3 presents the detailed error analysis module. Each detected violation is classified
by category and severity level, accompanied by a contextual explanation and recommended
corrective action. The structured breakdown enables users to systematically address formatting
inconsistencies while understanding the academic rule being enforced.
Figure 4 demonstrates the automatically generated annotated PDF output. The system high-
lights formatting violations directly within the manuscript, enabling visual correlation between
reported errors and their precise locations in the document. This feature significantly enhances
usability by bridging textual feedback with contextual visualization.
Figure 2: Document-Level Compliance Analysis Generated Immediately After Upload
25

Figure 3: Detailed Categorized Error Analysis with Contextual Explanation and Recommended
Corrections
Figure 4: Annotated PDF Output Highlighting Formatting Violations Within the Original
Manuscript

5
Introduction
Fix my Paper is a web-based intelligent academic formatting verification platform that is de-
signed to automatically analyze research papers and detect structural, syntactic, citation, and
formatting-related errors. Academic writing in technical domains follows strict formatting stan-
dards prescribed by institutions and journals like IEEE, APA etc. These standards ensure clar-
ity, reproducibility, and professional presentation of research work.
However, undergraduate students often lack formal training in academic writing conven-
tions. While they may possess strong technical knowledge, they frequently make formatting
mistakes such as:
• Improper ordering of figures and tables
• Missing figure/table captions
• Equations without numbering or explanation
• Incorrect citation styles
• Mismatched in-text citations and reference lists
• Inconsistent typography and notation
Most of the writing tools that currently exist primarily focus on grammar and language
improvement, offering little support for technical formatting compliance. Fix my Paper helps
bridge this gap by integrating rule-based validation and machine learning techniques to generate
structured, actionable improvement reports within seconds.
6
Motivation
The motivation for developing our project arises from the growing importance of academic
writing quality in technical education. Students with technical backgrounds are increasingly
required to produce research papers, project reports, and theses that adhere to strict formatting
standards defined by academic institutions and journals. While students often possess strong
technical knowledge, they frequently lack adequate training in academic writing conventions,
resulting in structural and formatting inconsistencies that negatively affect the clarity and pro-
fessionalism of their work.
A key motivating factor is the limited time available for faculty members to provide detailed
formatting feedback. Professors must manage multiple responsibilities, including teaching, re-
search, and administrative duties, which restricts their ability to thoroughly review formatting
aspects of every student submission. As a result, many formatting errors persist across sub-
missions, slowing students’ development of academic writing skills and increasing the overall
review burden.
Additionally, existing writing support tools primarily focus on grammar correction and lan-
guage enhancement, offering minimal assistance for technical formatting issues . Tools that
do address citation management often operate independently and do not verify contextual cor-
rectness within the document. This fragmented support creates a gap in automated academic
quality assurance for technical papers.
The need for an affordable and student-friendly automated solution further strengthens the
motivation behind this project. Many available platforms are subscription-based and may not
be accessible to all students. Therefore, a lightweight and specialized platform capable of
delivering fast, actionable feedback on technical formatting can significantly improve student
productivity, reduce faculty workload, and enhance overall research paper quality.
7
Literature Review
0.1
Literature Review
The domain of academic writing assistance has evolved significantly with the advancement
of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and machine learning techniques. Existing solutions
in this area can broadly be categorized into three groups: grammar and language correction
tools, plagiarism detection systems, and citation/reference management software. While these
systems contribute to improving academic writing quality, they operate in isolation and do not
provide comprehensive structural and formatting compliance verification tailored for technical
research papers.
0.1.1
Grammar and Language Correction Tools
Grammar correction platforms such as Grammarly, LanguageTool, and Writefull utilize NLP-
based models trained on large textual corpora to detect grammatical, lexical, and stylistic er-
rors. Recent research in Grammatical Error Correction (GEC) demonstrates the effectiveness
of transformer-based models in improving sentence fluency and readability.
However, these tools primarily treat documents as unstructured text and focus on linguistic
refinement rather than academic formatting compliance. They do not verify figure and table
sequencing, caption placement, equation numbering consistency, citation-reference mapping,
DOI validation, or journal-specific structural guidelines. Consequently, while they improve lan-
guage quality, they fail to address technical formatting requirements essential for engineering
research documents.
8
0.1.2
Plagiarism Detection Systems
Plagiarism detection systems compare textual similarity against large academic databases to
ensure originality. These platforms play a critical role in maintaining academic integrity. How-
ever, their objective is limited to similarity detection and originality verification.
They do not analyze structural correctness, formatting consistency, citation integrity, equa-
tion referencing, or document layout compliance. Therefore, plagiarism tools cannot serve as
comprehensive academic formatting verification systems.
0.1.3
Citation and Reference Management Tools
Reference management tools such as Zotero and Mendeley assist researchers in generating
citations in various formats, including IEEE and APA styles. These tools simplify bibliography
creation and reference organization.
Despite their usefulness, they operate as standalone utilities and do not verify contextual
correctness within the document. They do not ensure one-to-one mapping between in-text cita-
tions and reference list entries, detect missing references, validate DOI availability, check order-
ing consistency, or enforce journal-specific formatting constraints across the entire manuscript.
0.1.4
Research Gap Identification
A comprehensive review of existing literature and tools reveals a critical gap: there is no
lightweight, integrated system specifically designed to perform structural validation, citation
verification, equation compliance checks, figure-table consistency validation, and typography
analysis within a single unified platform.
Most available systems focus on isolated aspects of writing improvement rather than holistic
academic compliance verification. This limitation creates the need for an intelligent, automated
solution capable of analyzing technical manuscripts as structured academic artifacts rather than
plain text documents.
0.1.5
Comparative Analysis with Existing Solutions
Table 1 presents a structured comparison between existing tools and the proposed system.
9

0.1.6
Discussion
The comparative evaluation clearly demonstrates that existing tools operate in silos, address-
ing either grammar correction, citation generation, or originality verification independently.
None provide an integrated compliance framework capable of analyzing structural formatting,
citation integrity, equation consistency, and contextual integration simultaneously.
The proposed system differentiates itself by treating academic manuscripts as structured
technical documents. By combining rule-based validation mechanisms with machine learning-
based contextual verification, the system delivers comprehensive, fast, and actionable compli-
ance feedback tailored specifically for engineering and scientific research papers.
10
Problem Statement
Undergraduate and postgraduate students in technical disciplines are required to prepare re-
search papers and project reports that follow strict academic formatting standards prescribed
by universities, conferences, and journals. However, many students lack formal training in
technical writing and formatting conventions, which results in frequent structural and stylistic
errors in their submissions. These errors include improper figure and table referencing, miss-
ing or incorrectly placed captions, unnumbered or unexplained equations, inconsistent citation
styles, and mismatches between in-text citations and the reference list.
The absence of timely and detailed feedback further aggravates the problem. Faculty mem-
bers often face a heavy workload and are unable to provide comprehensive formatting correc-
tions for every submission. As a result, students repeatedly make similar mistakes, leading to
poor document quality, increased review cycles, and delays in evaluation or publication.
Existing writing assistance tools primarily focus on grammar and language improvement
and offer limited support for technical formatting verification. Tools that handle citations or
references work in isolation and do not validate the overall structural consistency of the doc-
ument. Consequently, there is a lack of an integrated, automated solution that can analyze
multiple components of a technical paper, including text structure, figures, tables, equations,
and references.
Therefore, there is a need for a comprehensive and automated system that can accurately
detect formatting inconsistencies, structural errors, and reference-related issues in technical
academic documents and provide actionable feedback to help students improve the quality and
compliance of their research papers.
11
Objectives
The primary objective of our project is to develop an intelligent, automated system capable of
verifying the structural and formatting correctness of technical research papers. The platform is
designed to go beyond traditional grammar-checking tools by focusing specifically on academic
formatting standards required in engineering and scientific writing. The purpose of this project
is to ensure that research documents comply with institutional and journal-specific guidelines
prior to submission.
Figure 1: System Architecture of Fix my Paper
One of the key objectives is to automatically detect formatting inconsistencies related to
figures, tables, and equations. This includes verifying whether figures and tables are properly
numbered, correctly referenced in the text, placed after their first mention, and accompanied
by appropriate captions. For mathematical expressions, the system aims to check sequential
equation numbering, proper formatting of variables and units, and whether all symbols are
defined clearly within the text.
12
Another important objective is to validate citation and reference integrity. The system seeks
to ensure that in-text citations correctly correspond to entries in the reference list, that refer-
ences are complete and properly ordered, and that citation styles such as IEEE or APA are
consistently applied. By automating these checks, the platform reduces the likelihood of sub-
mission rejections due to referencing errors.
The project also aims to reduce manual correction effort for both students and faculty mem-
bers. By generating structured and actionable feedback reports within a short processing time,
the system encourages students to independently refine their work before submission. This
objective directly contributes to improving overall academic writing quality and promoting
self-learning.
From a methodological perspective, the system follows a structured processing pipeline.
The process begins when a user uploads a research paper in PDF or DOC format. The backend
then extracts textual content, figures, tables, equations, and references using document parsing
utilities.
Following extraction, a rule-based engine employing regular expressions and pattern-matching
techniques analyzes structural formatting aspects. These rules are designed to detect errors such
as skipped numbering sequences, incorrect caption placement, improper punctuation, inconsis-
tent typography, and citation bracket formatting issues.
In parallel, machine learning models are applied to analyze the references section more
intelligently. These models assist in identifying incomplete references, mismatches between
in-text citations and bibliography entries, DOI validation issues, and inconsistencies in citation
style formatting. The combination of rule-based logic and machine learning enhances both
accuracy and contextual understanding.
Finally, the detected issues are aggregated into a structured analysis report. The report cat-
egorizes errors by severity and type, provides specific suggestions for correction, and may in-
clude an overall formatting compliance score. This report is then displayed through an intuitive
web interface, allowing users to easily review and implement the suggested improvements.
Through this systematic objective-driven methodology, our project aims to deliver a com-
prehensive, efficient, and user-friendly solution for technical academic formatting verification.
13
Simulation Platform and Requirements
The simulation platform for the project is designed to replicate a real-world academic docu-
ment processing environment where research papers are uploaded, analyzed, and evaluated for
formatting compliance.
The validation checks implemented in the simulation environment are listed in Table 2.
Table 2: Stage-1 Validation Checks Used in Simulation
#
Category
Checkable Requirement
1
Figures & Tables
Every figure and table is cited in the main text
2
Figures & Tables
No figure or table appears without textual reference
3
Figures
Figure captions are placed below the figure
4
Tables
Table captions are placed above the table
5
Figures & Tables
Figures and tables are numbered sequentially (no
skipping)
6
Figures & Tables
Figures and tables appear after first mention in text
7
Tables
Units are specified in all column headers
8
Figures & Tables

9
Equations

10
Equations

11
Equations
Every equation is referenced in the text
12

use
13
Math Formatting
Variables are italicized
14
Math Formatting

14
#
Category
Checkable Requirement
15
Math Formatting
Proper punctuation is used after equations
16
Citations
Citation style matches the target journal (IEEE/A-
PA/etc.)
17
Citations
Bracket punctuation is correct (e.g., [1], not .[1])
18
Citations
One-to-one match between in-text citations and refer-
ence list
19
References
No missing or uncited references
20
References
References are ordered correctly
21
References

pages)
22

DOI included wherever available
23

24

URLs and DOIs are valid and unbroken
25

vant)
26
Acronyms

27
Units

10 kg)
28
Typography
En-dash used for numeric ranges (e.g., 10–20)
29
Typography
Consistent spacing after commas and full stops
30
Consistency
Notation, symbols, and terminology used consistently
The frontend of the platform is built using modern web technologies such as React.js or
Next.js, combined with CSS for responsive and clean user interface design. The purpose of the
frontend is to provide a smooth document upload experience, display structured error reports,
and ensure intuitive navigation. The interface is designed to clearly categorize detected issues
under sections such as figures, equations, citations, references, and typography, making it easy
for users to understand and correct errors.
15
The backend of the system is implemented using Node.js or Python with FastAPI. The
backend handles core processing tasks, including document parsing, rule-based validation, ma-
chine learning inference, and report generation. FastAPI is particularly suitable due to its high
performance, asynchronous capabilities, and compatibility with machine learning workflows.
The backend acts as the central processing unit of the platform, coordinating data extraction,
analysis, and structured output generation.
For document analysis and machine learning operations, we have used Python as the pri-
mary programming language due to its rich ecosystem of libraries. Regular expressions and
rule-based engines are employed to detect formatting patterns such as figure numbering, cita-
tion brackets, equation formatting, and punctuation consistency. In addition, Natural Language
Processing techniques and Scikit-learn models assist in more complex tasks such as citation-
reference matching, reference completeness verification, and DOI validation. This hybrid ap-
proach ensures both speed and contextual intelligence in error detection.
The system requires a reliable database layer, such as PostgreSQL or MongoDB, to manage
user session data, analysis results, and system logs temporarily. While uploaded documents are
not stored permanently in order to maintain privacy, temporary storage ensures smooth report
generation and retrieval. Data deletion policies are implemented to remove documents after
processing, ensuring compliance with privacy and academic integrity standards.
From an infrastructure perspective, the platform is deployed on a cloud-based virtual ma-
chine capable of handling document uploads and concurrent processing requests. Containeriza-
tion using Docker ensures portability and consistent deployment across environments. Object
storage is utilized for temporary file management, while monitoring and logging services main-
tain system reliability, uptime tracking, and performance diagnostics.
For machine learning model training and experimentation, a short-term GPU-enabled com-
pute instance may be required. This is particularly useful during development and optimization
phases to improve model accuracy in reference classification and pattern recognition. Once
trained, inference can be performed efficiently on standard CPU-based servers to reduce oper-
ational costs.
Security and accessibility are also essential requirements of the platform. A registered do-
16
main name and SSL certificate are necessary to ensure secure HTTPS communication between
users and the server. Authentication mechanisms can be integrated in later stages to support
institutional adoption and personalized report tracking.
Overall, the simulation platform integrates modern web technologies, cloud infrastructure,
and machine learning frameworks to create a scalable and efficient academic formatting veri-
fication system. The combination of frontend usability, backend computational efficiency, and
secure deployment ensures that the system can operate reliably under real-world academic us-
age conditions.
17
Proposed Method
The proposed system is designed to automatically analyze technical research documents and
identify structural, formatting, grammatical, and reference-related issues using a hybrid archi-
tecture that combines rule-based validation, regular expressions, Python-based logical checks,
Optical Character Recognition (OCR), and machine learning models. The complete methodol-
ogy is described below.
0.1.7
Document Upload and Input Handling
The process begins with the user uploading a research paper through a web-based interface.
The system accepts documents in PDF or DOC format. Once uploaded, the file is securely
stored and passed to the backend processing pipeline. The interface is designed to be intuitive
and efficient, enabling rapid submission and automated evaluation.
0.1.8
OCR-Based Text Extraction and JSON Structuring
To ensure accurate structural analysis, the system implements an OCR-based processing pipeline
for PDF documents.
• The uploaded PDF is first processed using OCR to extract textual and layout information.
• The extracted content is then converted into a structured JSON representation containing
hierarchical components such as headings, paragraphs, figures, tables, equations, cap-
tions, citations, and references.
• This JSON structure preserves positional metadata (page number, bounding boxes, or-
dering), enabling precise structural validation.
This intermediate JSON representation serves as the core data structure for downstream
validation modules.
18
0.1.9
Structural and Formatting Validation Using Regex and Python Checks
The system applies a rule-based validation engine primarily implemented using:
• Regular Expressions (Regex)
• Python-based deterministic logical checks
Regex-based validation is used for detecting:
• Figure and table numbering patterns
• Caption placement (above/below validation)
• Equation numbering formats
• Citation bracket structure (e.g., [1], (1))
• Typography consistency patterns
Python logical checks ensure:
• Sequential numbering without skipping
• One-to-one mapping between mentions and objects
• Correct ordering of figures and tables after first reference
• Presence of defined symbols and units
This rule-driven approach ensures strict compliance with deterministic academic formatting
standards.
0.1.10
Machine Learning-Based Citation and Reference Analysis
While structural components rely primarily on regex and rule-based checks, reference valida-
tion is handled using a machine learning model.
The ML model performs:
• Reference completeness verification (authors, title, venue, year, pages)
19
• DOI detection and validation pattern recognition
• Citation style classification (IEEE, APA, etc.)
• Detection of mismatches between in-text citations and bibliography entries
• Identification of incomplete or inconsistent references
The model is trained to recognize structured reference patterns and contextual inconsisten-
cies that cannot be reliably captured using rule-based methods alone. This makes the reference
validation module the most ML-intensive component of the system.
0.1.11
Grammar and Syntax Correction Module
In addition to formatting compliance, the system incorporates grammar and syntax correction.
This module applies NLP-based correction techniques to detect:
• Grammatical inconsistencies
• Sentence-level syntax errors
• Punctuation errors
• Improper academic tone structures
The integration of grammar validation ensures that the final manuscript satisfies both struc-
tural compliance and professional language standards.
0.1.12
Contextual and Multi-Modal Validation
The system performs contextual validation to ensure that technical elements are properly inte-
grated within the manuscript. It verifies:
• Whether every equation is referenced in surrounding text
• Whether figures and tables are explained contextually
• Whether acronyms are defined at first occurrence
This stage moves beyond pattern matching and evaluates semantic integration within the
document structure.
20
0.1.13
Annotated PDF Reconstruction
After all validations are completed:
• The structured JSON representation is enriched with detected error metadata.
• The system reprocesses the document using OCR-based positional mapping.
• An annotated PDF is generated by overlaying highlights and comments at precise coor-
dinates corresponding to detected violations.
This PDF →JSON →Annotated PDF pipeline ensures traceability between detected issues
and their exact visual location in the manuscript.
0.1.14
Report Generation and Feedback
The system generates a structured compliance report categorizing errors by:
• Category (Figures, Tables, Equations, Citations, References, Typography, Grammar)
• Severity level
• Corrective recommendation
The analysis process is optimized for performance and produces results within approxi-
mately 40 seconds per document under standard computational settings.
0.1.15
System Benefits
The hybrid architecture combining:
• Regex-based rule validation
• Python logical checks
• Machine learning-based reference intelligence
• OCR-based structured reconstruction
21
ensures high precision, contextual reliability, and scalability. The system is lightweight,
cloud-deployable, and cost-effective for students. It significantly reduces manual formatting
effort while minimizing repetitive faculty workload by automating non-conceptual compliance
verification tasks.
22
Conclusion
The project “An Intelligent System for Academic Manuscript Formatting and Compliance” has
successfully progressed from conceptual design to a fully functional and deployable prototype.
The system is now operational in a real-world environment, with the frontend successfully
deployed on Vercel and the backend deployed on Render, ensuring scalability, reliability, and
secure cloud-based access.
To validate real-world applicability, the system was rigorously tested on 20 research pa-
pers sourced from leading Indian Institutes of Technology (IITs). The testing phase confirmed
that the platform effectively detects structural inconsistencies, citation mismatches, equation
formatting issues, figure-table misplacement, and typography errors in technical manuscripts.
The evaluation demonstrated that the system is capable of automatically identifying and report-
ing formatting violations across all 30 validation checks listed in the simulation framework.
A major milestone achieved during this phase is that the system currently eliminates all
grammar and syntax errors identified in the validation table, thereby ensuring that submitted
manuscripts meet professional writing standards in addition to structural compliance. This
significantly strengthens the practical reliability of the platform.
Furthermore, the project has received 25,000 rupees in research funding from Netaji Subhas
University of Technology (NSUT) to support advanced prototype development, model refine-
ment, and scalability enhancements. This institutional backing validates both the technical
feasibility and academic significance of the system.
The hybrid architecture combining rule-based validation with machine learning models has
proven to be highly effective. Rule-based logic ensures strict adherence to deterministic aca-
demic standards (numbering sequences, caption positioning, citation brackets), while machine
learning models intelligently validate contextual relationships such as citation-reference map-
23
ping, DOI verification, and reference completeness.
From an infrastructure perspective, successful cloud deployment confirms that the system is
production-ready and capable of handling real-time document analysis requests. The process-
ing time remains optimized at approximately 40 seconds per document, demonstrating both
efficiency and scalability.
Key Outcomes Achieved
• Technical feasibility through successful cloud deployment
• Functional reliability through IIT paper testing
• Writing accuracy through grammar and syntax elimination
• Institutional validation through research funding support
• Scalability ensured through production-ready infrastructure
With continued refinement and expanded dataset validation, the system has strong poten-
tial for institutional integration within universities as a mandatory pre-submission compliance
tool. In the long term, the platform can evolve into a standardized academic quality assurance
framework capable of improving research submission standards at scale.
Prototype Demonstration
Figure 2, Figure 3 and Figure 4 illustrate the deployed prototype interface and the structured
compliance report generated by the system.
Prototype Demonstration and System Output
To validate the practical implementation of the proposed framework, the deployed prototype
was evaluated through real-time document uploads and automated compliance analysis. The
system provides a structured multi-stage output consisting of document-level validation, de-
tailed categorized error explanations, and an annotated PDF highlighting detected violations.
24
Figure 2 illustrates the initial system response upon document upload. The interface presents
a high-level compliance summary, categorizes detected issues (figures, equations, citations, ref-
erences, typography), and generates an overall formatting assessment score. This stage con-
firms successful parsing, structural extraction, and rule-engine execution.
Figure 3 presents the detailed error analysis module. Each detected violation is classified
by category and severity level, accompanied by a contextual explanation and recommended
corrective action. The structured breakdown enables users to systematically address formatting
inconsistencies while understanding the academic rule being enforced.
Figure 4 demonstrates the automatically generated annotated PDF output. The system high-
lights formatting violations directly within the manuscript, enabling visual correlation between
reported errors and their precise locations in the document. This feature significantly enhances
usability by bridging textual feedback with contextual visualization.
Figure 2: Document-Level Compliance Analysis Generated Immediately After Upload
25
Figure 3: Detailed Categorized Error Analysis with Contextual Explanation and Recommended
Corrections
Figure 4: Annotated PDF Output Highlighting Formatting Violations Within the Original
Manuscript

0.1.9Structural and Formatting Validation Using Regex and Python Checks.19
formatting-related errors. Academic writing in technical domains follows strict formatting stan-
dards prescribed by institutions and journals like IEEE, APA etc.These standards ensure clar-
ity, reproducibility, and professional presentation of research work.

Additionally, existing writing support tools primarily focus on grammar correction and lan-
do address citation management often operate independently and do not verify contextual cor-
The need for an affordable and student-friendly automated solution further strengthens the
motivation behind this project.Many available platforms are subscription-based and may not

tools, plagiarism detection systems, and citation/reference management software.While these

Grammar correction platforms such as Grammarly, LanguageTool, and Writefull utilize NLP-
rors.Recent research in Grammatical Error Correction (GEC) demonstrates the effectiveness
of transformer-based models in improving sentence fluency and readability.
However, these tools primarily treat documents as unstructured text and focus on linguistic
DOI validation, or journal-specific structural guidelines. Consequently, while they improve lan-
guage quality,they fail to address technical formatting requirements essential for engineering
ensure originality.These platforms play a critical role in maintaining academic integrity.How-
ever, their objective is limited to similarity detection and originality verification.
They do not analyze structural correctness, formatting consistency, citation integrity, equa-
tion referencing, or document layout compliance.Therefore, plagiarism tools cannot serve as
citations in various formats, including IEEE and APA styles.These tools simplify bibliography
correctness within the document.They do not ensure one-to-one mapping between in-text cita-
tions and reference list entries, detect missing references, validate DOI availability, check order-
ing consistency, or enforce journal-specific formatting constraints across the entire manuscript.
analysis within a single unified platform.
Most available systems focus on isolated aspects of writing improvement rather than holistic
academic compliance verification. This limitation creates the need for an intelligent, automated
solution capable of analyzing technical manuscripts as structured academic artifacts rather than

ing or incorrectly placed captions, unnumbered or unexplained equations, inconsistent citation

The absence of timely and detailed feedback further aggravates the problem.Faculty mem-
bers often face a heavy workload and are unable to provide comprehensive formatting correc-
tions for every submission.As a result, students repeatedly make similar mistakes, leading to
poor document quality, increased review cycles, and delays in evaluation or publication.

The primary objective of our project is to develop an intelligent, automated system capable of
verifying the structural and formatting correctness of technical research papers. The platform is
designed to go beyond traditional grammar-checking tools by focusing specifically on academic
formatting standards required in engineering and scientific writing.The purpose of this project
is to ensure that research documents comply with institutional and journal-specific guidelines
Figure 1:System Architecture of Fix my Paper
figures, tables, and equations.This includes verifying whether figures and tables are properly
Another important objective is to validate citation and reference integrity. The system seeks
consistently applied.By automating these checks, the platform reduces the likelihood of sub-
mission rejections due to referencing errors.
The project also aims to reduce manual correction effort for both students and faculty mem-
bers.By generating structured and actionable feedback reports within a short processing time,
The process begins when a user uploads a research paper in PDF or DOC format.The backend
then extracts textual content, figures, tables, equations, and references using document parsing
Following extraction, a rule-based engine employing regular expressions and pattern-matching
techniques analyzes structural formatting aspects. These rules are designed to detect errors such
as skipped numbering sequences, incorrect caption placement, improper punctuation, inconsis-
tent typography, and citation bracket formatting issues.
in-text citations and bibliography entries, DOI validation issues, and inconsistencies in citation
Finally, the detected issues are aggregated into a structured analysis report.The report cat-
egorizes errors by severity and type, provides specific suggestions for correction, and may in-
clude an overall formatting compliance score. This report is then displayed through an intuitive
web interface, allowing users to easily review and implement the suggested improvements.
Through this systematic objective-driven methodology,our project aims to deliver a com-
prehensive, efficient, and user-friendly solution for technical academic formatting verification.

The validation checks implemented in the simulation environment are listed in Table 2.
Table 2:Stage-1 Validation Checks Used in Simulation
1Figures & TablesEvery figure and table is cited in the main text
2Figures & TablesNo figure or table appears without textual reference
3FiguresFigure captions are placed below the figure
4TablesTable captions are placed above the table
6Figures & TablesFigures and tables appear after first mention in text
7TablesUnits are specified in all column headers
8Figures & TablesImages are clear, readable, and high resolution
10EquationsEquation numbers are in parentheses and flush right
11EquationsEvery equation is referenced in the text
14Math FormattingFunctions and units are in roman (non-italic) font
15Math FormattingProper punctuation is used after equations
18CitationsOne-to-one match between in-text citations and refer-
24ReferencesURLs and DOIs are valid and unbroken
29TypographyConsistent spacing after commas and full stops

rule-based engines are employed to detect formatting patterns such as figure numbering, cita-

reference matching,reference completeness verification, and DOI validation.This hybrid ap-

The system requires a reliable database layer, such as PostgreSQL or MongoDB, to manage
user session data, analysis results, and system logs temporarily.While uploaded documents are
not stored permanently in order to maintain privacy, temporary storage ensures smooth report
processing, ensuring compliance with privacy and academic integrity standards.
From an infrastructure perspective,the platform is deployed on a cloud-based virtual ma-
chine capable of handling document uploads and concurrent processing requests. Containeriza-
tion using Docker ensures portability and consistent deployment across environments.Object
storage is utilized for temporary file management, while monitoring and logging services main-
tain system reliability, uptime tracking, and performance diagnostics.
For machine learning model training and experimentation, a short-term GPU-enabled com-
pute instance may be required.This is particularly useful during development and optimization
trained, inference can be performed efficiently on standard CPU-based servers to reduce oper-
Security and accessibility are also essential requirements of the platform.A registered do-
main name and SSL certificate are necessary to ensure secure HTTPS communication between
institutional adoption and personalized report tracking.
Overall, the simulation platform integrates modern web technologies, cloud infrastructure,
and machine learning frameworks to create a scalable and efficient academic formatting veri-
fication system.The combination of frontend usability, backend computational efficiency, and
secure deployment ensures that the system can operate reliably under real-world academic us-
identify structural, formatting, grammatical, and reference-related issues using a hybrid archi-
tecture that combines rule-based validation, regular expressions, Python-based logical checks,
Optical Character Recognition (OCR), and machine learning models.The complete methodol-

To ensure accurate structural analysis, the system implements an OCR-based processing pipeline
•The uploaded PDF is first processed using OCR to extract textual and layout information.

0.1.9Structural and Formatting Validation Using Regex and Python Checks
The system applies a rule-based validation engine primarily implemented using:
Regex-based validation is used for detecting:
•Correct ordering of figures and tables after first reference
This rule-driven approach ensures strict compliance with deterministic academic formatting
While structural components rely primarily on regex and rule-based checks, reference valida-
tion is handled using a machine learning model.
•Reference completeness verification (authors, title, venue, year, pages)
•Detection of mismatches between in-text citations and bibliography entries
The model is trained to recognize structured reference patterns and contextual inconsisten-
cies that cannot be reliably captured using rule-based methods alone.This makes the reference
validation module the most ML-intensive component of the system.
In addition to formatting compliance, the system incorporates grammar and syntax correction.
This module applies NLP-based correction techniques to detect:
The integration of grammar validation ensures that the final manuscript satisfies both struc-
tural compliance and professional language standards.
The system performs contextual validation to ensure that technical elements are properly inte-
•Whether every equation is referenced in surrounding text
•Whether figures and tables are explained contextually
•Whether acronyms are defined at first occurrence
•The structured JSON representation is enriched with detected error metadata.
•The system reprocesses the document using OCR-based positional mapping.
•An annotated PDF is generated by overlaying highlights and comments at precise coor-
This PDF →JSON →Annotated PDF pipeline ensures traceability between detected issues
and their exact visual location in the manuscript.
The system generates a structured compliance report categorizing errors by:
•Category (Figures, Tables, Equations, Citations, References, Typography, Grammar)
mately 40 seconds per document under standard computational settings.
